papers.title: A Deep Learning Based Automatic Defect Analysis Framework for In-situ TEM Ion Irradiations
papers.abstract: Videos captured using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) can encode details regarding the morphological and temporal evolution of a material by taking snapshots of the microstructure sequentially. However, manual analysis of such video is tedious, error-prone, unreliable, and prohibitively time-consuming if one wishes to analyze a significant fraction of frames for even videos of modest length. In this work, we developed an automated TEM video analysis system for microstructural features based on the advanced object detection model called YOLO and tested the system on an in-situ ion irradiation TEM video of dislocation loops formed in a FeCrAl alloy. The system provides analysis of features observed in TEM including both static and dynamic properties using the YOLO-based defect detection module coupled to a geometry analysis module and a dynamic tracking module. Results show that the system can achieve human comparable performance with an F1 score of 0.89 for fast, consistent, and scalable frame-level defect analysis. This result is obtained on a real but exceptionally clean and stable data set and more challenging data sets may not achieve this performance. The dynamic tracking also enabled evaluation of individual defect evolution like per defect growth rate at a fidelity never before achieved using common human analysis methods. Our work shows that automatically detecting and tracking interesting microstructures and properties contained in TEM videos is viable and opens new doors for evaluating materials dynamics.

papers.title: Multi defect detection and analysis of electron microscopy images with deep learning
papers.abstract: Electron microscopy is widely used to explore defects in crystal structures, but human detecting of defects is often time-consuming, error-prone, and unreliable, and is not scalable to large numbers of images or real-time analysis. In this work, we discuss the application of machine learning approaches to find the location and geometry of different defect clusters in irradiated steels. We show that a deep learning based Faster R-CNN analysis system has a performance comparable to human analysis with relatively small training data sets. This study proves the promising ability to apply deep learning to assist the development of automated microscopy data analysis even when multiple features are present and paves the way for fast, scalable, and reliable analysis systems for massive amounts of modern electron microscopy data.

papers.title: Diamond crystal with Y-defects: spectroscopy and transmittion electron microscopy
papers.abstract: The paper presents resutls of investigation of a natural Ib-IaA diamond containing Y-defects. Analysis of spatial distribution of nitrogen-related A and C centers and intensity of Infra-red absorption at Raman frequency (1332 cm-1) reveals anticorrelation between these defects. Transmission electron microscopy of a zone with abundant Y-defects shows presence of dislocations in various configurations and numerous clusters of point defects generated by non-conservative dislocation motion. Extended defects with shape resembling thin (1-3 nm) rhombic plates with the largest dimension up to 20 nm are observed. Analysis of contrast of these defects shows that they represent nanosized voids (vacancy clusters). It is suggested that the defects were formed by annihilation of dislocation dipoles with subsequent growth by consumption of vacancies produced by non-conservative motion of dislocations. Upon excitation by 787 nm laser, numerous narrow photoluminescecne lines are observed between 800-900 nm; their intensity and position show irregular temporal variations. Such behaviour (blinking) was earlier noted for hydrogenated nanodiamonds. It is suggested that unusual behaviour of the luminescence lines may be explained by recombination processes at internal walls of the discovered nanovoids.

papers.title: Deep Denoising For Scientific Discovery: A Case Study In Electron Microscopy
papers.abstract: Denoising is a fundamental challenge in scientific imaging. Deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) provide the current state of the art in denoising natural images, where they produce impressive results. However, their potential has barely been explored in the context of scientific imaging. Denoising CNNs are typically trained on real natural images artificially corrupted with simulated noise. In contrast, in scientific applications, noiseless ground-truth images are usually not available. To address this issue, we propose a simulation-based denoising (SBD) framework, in which CNNs are trained on simulated images. We test the framework on data obtained from transmission electron microscopy (TEM), an imaging technique with widespread applications in material science, biology, and medicine. SBD outperforms existing techniques by a wide margin on a simulated benchmark dataset, as well as on real data. Apart from the denoised images, SBD generates likelihood maps to visualize the agreement between the structure of the denoised image and the observed data. Our results reveal shortcomings of state-of-the-art denoising architectures, such as their small field-of-view: substantially increasing the field-of-view of the CNNs allows them to exploit non-local periodic patterns in the data, which is crucial at high noise levels. In addition, we analyze the generalization capability of SBD, demonstrating that the trained networks are robust to variations of imaging parameters and of the underlying signal structure. Finally, we release the first publicly available benchmark dataset of TEM images, containing 18,000 examples.

papers.title: Data-Driven Electron Microscopy: Electron Diffraction Imaging of Materials Structural Properties
papers.abstract: Transmission electron diffraction is a powerful and versatile structural probe for the characterization of a broad range of materials, from nanocrystalline thin films to single crystals. With recent developments in fast electron detectors and efficient computer algorithms, it now becomes possible to collect unprecedently large datasets of diffraction patterns (DPs) and process DPs to extract crystallographic information to form images or tomograms based on crystal structural properties, giving rise to data-driven electron microscopy. Critical to this kind of imaging is the type of crystallographic information being collected, which can be achieved with a judicious choice of electron diffraction techniques, and the efficiency and accuracy of DP processing, which requires the development of new algorithms. Here, we review recent progress made in data collection, new algorithms, and automated electron DP analysis. These progresses will be highlighted using application examples in materials research. Future opportunities based on smart sampling and machine learning are also discussed.

existing_examples.name: Deep learning for materials science research
existing_examples.description: Numerous research papers and projects have explored using CNNs and other deep learning techniques for analyzing TEM images to identify and classify various material features, including crystal defects. This is a very active and well-established research area.

existing_examples.name: Automated defect analysis software (e.g., DigitalMicrograph scripts, custom solutions)
existing_examples.description: While not always using deep learning, there are existing software tools and custom scripts developed for TEM image analysis that perform automated or semi-automated defect identification based on image processing techniques. The deep learning approach is an evolution of this.

existing_examples.name: Specific research papers and open-source projects
existing_examples.description: Many specific examples exist, such as 'Deep learning for defect detection and classification in atomic-resolution images' (e.g., by Ziatdinov et al.), 'Automated defect detection in TEM images using convolutional neural networks' (e.g., by Makovetsky et al.), and various GitHub repositories demonstrating similar implementations.

reality_check.assessment: The idea of using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to automatically identify and classify crystal defect types (vacancies, dislocations, grain boundaries) in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images is a well-established and active area of research in materials science and computational imaging. There are numerous published papers, open-source implementations, and ongoing projects that have demonstrated and implemented this exact concept. It's a standard application of deep learning in materials characterization.
novelty_aspects: Specific novel CNN architecture or training methodology that significantly outperforms existing methods.
Application to a very niche or challenging material system where existing methods struggle.
Integration into a real-time, in-situ TEM analysis pipeline with novel hardware/software co-design.

Development of a robust, user-friendly, and commercially viable software product that is widely adopted by the materials science community, potentially with novel visualization or interpretation tools.
reality_check.recommendation: While the core idea is not novel, there is still room for innovation. To make this a novel research contribution or product, you would need to focus on a specific, unique aspect. Consider: 1) Developing a significantly more accurate or efficient model for a particular challenging defect type or material. 2) Creating a robust, user-friendly, and well-validated software package that is superior to existing open-source or academic solutions. 3) Integrating this analysis into a broader materials design or manufacturing workflow. 4) Exploring explainable AI (XAI) techniques to provide deeper insights into defect formation mechanisms.

matched_sections.heading: Material and methods
matched_sections.text_snippet: The YOLOv3 model was adopted from an open GitHub repository (https://github.com/qqwweee/keras-yolo3). To train the model, we first converted the pretrained darknet53 weight via COCO dataset into Keras format and then modified the final class number to our defect number, which was one class in our case since we treated all a/2 〈 111 〉 and a 〈 100 〉 loops as the same type of defect.

matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: Electron microscopy (EM) is one of the most powerful tools for researchers to extract and collect micrometer down to angstrom scale structural and morphological properties of materials, including repeated structural units (e.g., unit cells of crystals) and defected regions (e.g., grain boundary, impurities, defect clusters). Traditionally, researchers have to manually label defects and repeatedly measure the relevant properties to obtain statistically meaningful values, which is time-consuming, error-prone, inconsistent, and hard to scale.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states the problem of manual labeling of defects like grain boundaries and defect clusters in EM images being time-consuming and error-prone, which aligns with the user's implicit problem statement of needing automatic identification and classification.

matched_sections.paper_title: Multi defect detection and analysis of electron microscopy images with deep learning
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: We show that a deep learning based Faster R-CNN analysis system has a performance comparable to human analysis with relatively small training data sets.
matched_sections.reason: The user proposes a 'convolutional neural network' which is a type of deep learning, and the paper uses a 'deep learning based Faster R-CNN analysis system' for defect detection, indicating a very similar methodological approach.

matched_sections.paper_title: Multi defect detection and analysis of electron microscopy images with deep learning
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: Recent developments (<10 years) in deep learning methods 17,18 have demonstrated that object detection in images can be automated with minimal hyperparameters and yield human or even better than human levels of performance. These frameworks are now being adapted towards finding defects in metals, and cavities, precipitates, and line dislocations 5,19-21 .
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea specifies a 'convolutional neural network' for identification and classification, which falls under the 'deep learning methods' and 'object detection in images' discussed in the paper for finding defects like dislocations.

matched_sections.paper_title: Multi defect detection and analysis of electron microscopy images with deep learning
matched_sections.heading: Conclusion
matched_sections.text_snippet: This study demonstrated a practical deep learning based automatic STEM image defect detection system implemented by incorporating Faster R-CNN for detection and watershed flood algorithm for geometry fitting.
matched_sections.reason: The user proposes a 'convolutional neural network' for defect identification and classification, which is a core component of the 'deep learning based automatic STEM image defect detection system' using Faster R-CNN described in the paper.

matched_sections.paper_title: Multi defect detection and analysis of electron microscopy images with deep learning
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: Electron microscopy is widely used to explore defects in crystal structures, but human detecting of defects is often time-consuming, error-prone, and unreliable, and is not scalable to large numbers of images or real-time analysis. In this work, we discuss the application of machine learning approaches to find the location and geometry of different defect clusters in irradiated steels.

matched_sections.paper_title: Diamond crystal with Y-defects: spectroscopy and transmittion electron microscopy
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: Transmission electron microscopy of a zone with abundant Y-defects shows presence of dislocations in various configurations and numerous clusters of point defects generated by non-conservative dislocation motion. Extended defects with shape resembling thin (1-3 nm) rhombic plates with the largest dimension up to 20 nm are observed. Analysis of contrast of these defects shows that they represent nanosized voids (vacancy clusters).

matched_sections.paper_title: Diamond crystal with Y-defects: spectroscopy and transmittion electron microscopy
matched_sections.heading: Dislocations
matched_sections.text_snippet: In the zone with the Y-defects numerous perfect and partial dislocations in various configurations as well as dislocation loops are observed (Fig. 6). Important feature of this crystal zone is the presence of numerous clusters of point defects, generated by nonconservative dislocation motion (dislocation debris). These defects lack noticeable concentrations of impurities and, presumably, largely consist of vacancies and interstitials.
matched_sections.reason: The paper extensively discusses dislocations and vacancy clusters (point defects) in crystals, which are direct crystal defect types mentioned in the user's idea.

matched_sections.paper_title: Diamond crystal with Y-defects: spectroscopy and transmittion electron microscopy
matched_sections.heading: Voids .
matched_sections.text_snippet: The largest dimension of these defects in the (111) plane reach 10-20 nm, but their thickness is not larger than 2-3 nm. These defects often form groups and may reside in intersecting {111} planes. Numerous attempts to detect impurities in these defects failed; we note that an idea that all the defects were opened during the sample preparation is clearly incorrect. The contrast variations in different imaging regimes point to low density of the entrapped material, thus, the defects could be described as agglomerations of vacancies.

matched_sections.paper_title: Diamond crystal with Y-defects: spectroscopy and transmittion electron microscopy
matched_sections.heading: Conclusions.
matched_sections.text_snippet: Transmission Electron Microscopy of foils extracted from a Ib-IaA natural diamond (Yubileinaya kimberlite pipe) containing Y-defects shows that the sample underwent plastic deformation at high temperatures. Numerous dislocations, dislocation dipoles and loops are present. It is shown that the dislocation motion was non-conservative, leading to formation of clusters of point defects. Absence of noticeable concentrations of impurities imply that the clusters mostly consist of vacancies and interstitials. Besides dislocations, extended defects with shapes that could be described as thin (1-3 nm) rhombic plates up to 5-20 nm in the largest dimension and lying in (intersecting) (111) planes. Analysis of contrast of the images and absence of impurities suggest that these objects represent nanosised voids (vacancy clusters).
matched_sections.reason: The paper's conclusions include the identification and characterization of dislocations and vacancy clusters (voids) using TEM, which aligns with the user's goal of identifying and classifying these defects, although the user proposes an automated method.

matched_sections.paper_title: Data-Driven Electron Microscopy: Electron Diffraction Imaging of Materials Structural Properties
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: Transmission electron diffraction is a powerful and versatile structural probe for the characterization of a broad range of materials, from nanocrystalline thin films to single crystals. With recent developments in fast electron detectors and efficient computer algorithms, it now becomes possible to collect unprecedently large datasets of diffraction patterns (DPs) and process DPs to extract crystallographic information to form images or tomograms based on crystal structural properties, giving rise to data-driven electron microscopy.
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper are firmly rooted in the domain of materials science, specifically using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) for characterizing crystal structures and defects.

matched_sections.paper_title: Data-Driven Electron Microscopy: Electron Diffraction Imaging of Materials Structural Properties
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: The background of this review is atomic structure determination using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In current practice, such analysis is primarily performed using atomic resolution imaging. At atomic scale, aberration corrected (scanning) TEM ((S)TEM) now routinely achieves sub-Å resolution for imaging single atoms [1, 2], defects in a diversity of hard materials [3-5], from ultra-thin 2D atomic layers [6-8] to nanoparticles and crystals [9-11] and more recently the structure of porous materials [12].
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper are firmly rooted in the domain of materials science, specifically using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) for characterizing crystal structures and defects.

matched_sections.paper_title: Data-Driven Electron Microscopy: Electron Diffraction Imaging of Materials Structural Properties
matched_sections.heading: Cepstral STEM using electron diffuse scattering
matched_sections.text_snippet: In crystals, a part of diffuse scattering is due to elastic scattering from structural imperfections, such as point defects, dislocations and stacking faults. Another source of diffuse scattering is partial ordering, for example short-range ordering in an alloy. The nature of diffuse scattering depends sensitively on its origin [29]. Thus, diffuse scattering signals can be used for determining crystal imperfections.
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea explicitly mentions 'crystal defect types including vacancies, dislocations, and grain boundaries'. The paper discusses 'structural imperfections, such as point defects, dislocations and stacking faults' and 'grain boundary and orientation imaging' as targets for analysis using electron microscopy, indicating a direct overlap in the specific application domain of defect characterization.

matched_sections.paper_title: Data-Driven Electron Microscopy: Electron Diffraction Imaging of Materials Structural Properties

matched_sections.text_snippet: In crystals, a part of diffuse scattering is due to elastic scattering from structural imperfections, such as point defects, dislocations and stacking faults. Another source of diffuse scattering is partial ordering, for example short-range ordering in an alloy. The nature of diffuse scattering depends sensitively on its origin [29]. Thus, diffuse scattering signals can be used for determining crystal imperfections.
matched_sections.reason: The user proposes to 'automatically identify and classify crystal defect types including vacancies, dislocations, and grain boundaries'. The paper discusses using diffuse scattering signals for 'determining crystal imperfections' and mentions 'dislocations' and 'grain boundary and orientation imaging'. While the paper doesn't explicitly state 'automatic classification' of all these types using a CNN, it points towards methods for identifying imperfections, which is a related contribution.

matched_sections.paper_title: Data-Driven Electron Microscopy: Electron Diffraction Imaging of Materials Structural Properties
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: At nm to micron scales, orientation, phase and strain mapping provides critical information about microstructure, including lattice rotation and deformation in the presence of defects and interfaces [22, 23, 25, 26, 28]. Electron diffuse scattering recorded in DPs also provides information about local disorder, from short-range order (SRO) to the presence of dislocations and stacking faults [11, 29, 30]. Ultimately, grain boundary and orientation imaging at mesoscopic scale can be combined with atomic and nanoscopic structural information for a complete determination of materials' atomic structure.
matched_sections.reason: The user proposes to 'automatically identify and classify crystal defect types including vacancies, dislocations, and grain boundaries'. The paper discusses using diffuse scattering signals for 'determining crystal imperfections' and mentions 'dislocations' and 'grain boundary and orientation imaging'. While the paper doesn't explicitly state 'automatic classification' of all these types using a CNN, it points towards methods for identifying imperfections, which is a related contribution.

linked_sections.paper_title: A Deep Learning Based Automatic Defect Analysis Framework for In-situ TEM Ion Irradiations
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: In this work, we developed an automated TEM video analysis system for microstructural features based on the advanced object detection model called YOLO and tested the system on an in-situ ion irradiation TEM video of dislocation loops formed in a FeCrAl alloy. The system provides analysis of features observed in TEM including both static and dynamic properties using the YOLO-based defect detection module coupled to a geometry analysis module and a dynamic tracking module.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper address the problem of automatically identifying and classifying crystal defects in TEM images/videos.

linked_sections.paper_title: A Deep Learning Based Automatic Defect Analysis Framework for In-situ TEM Ion Irradiations
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: In this work, we developed an automated TEM video analysis system for microstructural features based on the advanced object detection model called YOLO and tested the system on an in-situ ion irradiation TEM video of dislocation loops formed in a FeCrAl alloy.
linked_sections.reason: The user proposes a 'convolutional neural network' and the paper uses 'YOLO', which is a type of convolutional neural network for object detection. Both are trained on TEM images for defect identification.

linked_sections.paper_title: A Deep Learning Based Automatic Defect Analysis Framework for In-situ TEM Ion Irradiations
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: Videos captured using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) can encode details regarding the morphological and temporal evolution of a material by taking snapshots of the microstructure sequentially. However, manual analysis of such video is tedious, error-prone, unreliable, and prohibitively time-consuming if one wishes to analyze a significant fraction of frames for even videos of modest length.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper are focused on the analysis of Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images/videos.

linked_sections.paper_title: A Deep Learning Based Automatic Defect Analysis Framework for In-situ TEM Ion Irradiations
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: In this work, we developed an automated TEM video analysis system for microstructural features based on the advanced object detection model called YOLO and tested the system on an in-situ ion irradiation TEM video of dislocation loops formed in a FeCrAl alloy. The system provides analysis of features observed in TEM including both static and dynamic properties using the YOLO-based defect detection module coupled to a geometry analysis module and a dynamic tracking module.
linked_sections.reason: The paper's contribution is an automated system for defect analysis in TEM videos, which directly overlaps with the user's goal of automatically identifying and classifying crystal defect types.

layer2_full.summary: Your research idea shows significant overlap, particularly in the problem formulation and application domain, with existing work on defect analysis in electron microscopy images. To enhance originality, consider differentiating your core methodology or focusing on a more novel contribution beyond general defect detection.
